risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "participants were blocked on race/ethnicity and then randomly assigned within each race/ethnicity block to condition—treatment or alternative care—using the random number function in our subject database." Comment: This describes a clearly random allocation process, with blocking and a random number function, which is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random.

Quote: "participants were blocked on race/ethnicity and randomized within block into either (1) treatment intervention group ... or (2) alternative care group" and "using the random number function in our subject database. Emails indicating group assignment ... were auto-generated in the database and sent to participants" Comment: Although the paper does not explicitly say "allocation concealment," the assignment was generated within the subject database and group-assignment emails were auto-generated after randomization, which indicates a centralized, database-based allocation process that would have prevented recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Although research assistants were aware of group assignment, all other interactions with subjects were delivered by emails that were standardized across groups and fully automated to avoid differential interactions by group assignment. All other research team members were blinded and, aside from crisis calls, no research team members had direct interaction with subjects after randomization." Comment: Participants necessarily received different interventions (MoodHacker app vs links to depression websites) and could not be blinded to which program they were using; personnel delivering the intervention were also not fully blinded because research assistants were aware of group assignment. Because the main outcomes were self-reported depression symptoms and work-related measures that could plausibly be influenced by participants' and personnel's knowledge of allocation, the study is at high risk of performance bias.

Summary: Outcomes were assessed by participants themselves using online self-report measures ("Participants completed online follow-up self-assessments at 6 weeks and 10 weeks after baseline"; "Online surveys were used to assess... depression symptoms... worksite outcomes... user satisfaction"). The study also states that "research assistants were aware of group assignment," and there is no explicit statement that outcome assessment was blinded. Because the main outcomes were patient-reported and the patients knew their allocation to MoodHacker vs alternative care, knowledge of allocation could influence reporting of subjective outcomes such as depression symptoms, behavioral activation, negative thinking, and satisfaction. This creates a high risk of detection bias.

Quote: "Examination of attrition between pretest and 6-week follow-up revealed only 10 out of 150 (6.7%) treatment participants did not complete the assessment compared to 4 out of 150 (2.7%) alternative care participants. Only 10 out of 150 (6.7%) treatment participants did not complete the 10-week follow-up assessment compared to 5 out of 150 (3.3%) alternative care participants. Attrition rates did not significantly differ by condition. Moreover, we found no statistically significant differences in demographic characteristics or baseline outcomes by attrition status, nor did we find any statistically significant interactions between attrition and condition predicting baseline outcomes, suggesting that attrition was not systematic." Comment: Follow-up loss was low overall and balanced between groups at both time points, with no evidence that missingness was related to baseline characteristics or outcomes. The study also used ITT analyses and imputation, supporting a low risk judgement for attrition bias.
